evidence_locator: AUA Official History (auanet.org/about-us/aua-overview/history-of-the-aua); Crane & Bloom, J Urol 2010;184:1
notes: On February 22, 1902, eight members of the New York Genito-Urinary Society met at Guiteras' home and voted to disband the society and form the American Urological Association. Guiteras was elected first president. Harvard-trained (MD 1883) with postgraduate study in Paris, Vienna, and Berlin, he represented an independent New York surgical lineage with no Halsted connection. The AUA is now the largest urological organization in the world with over 23,000 members.

Young served as AUA president in 1909 and founded the Journal of Urology in 1917, serving as its first editor-in-chief until his death in 1945. The AUA absorbed the Journal in 1921 and it became the official AUA scientific publication. The AUA eventually headquartered at Young's Baltimore estate, physically and symbolically fusing the independent Guiteras-New York origin with the Halstedian Hopkins trunk. Young is universally regarded as the Father of Modern American Urology.
Young founded the Brady Urological Institute at Hopkins, establishing the first dedicated, modern residency model for urology.

evidence_locator: American Board of Urology Official History (abu.org/who-we-are)
notes: The ABU was organized in Chicago on September 24, 1934 and incorporated in Delaware on May 6, 1935. It was established through the combined efforts of the AUA, the American Association of Genito-Urinary Surgeons, and the AMA Section on Urology. The ABU is one of 24 ABMS member boards and certifies all urologists in the United States. This follows the established graph pattern of professional societies spawning certification boards (AOA → ABOS, ACS/ASA → ABS).

Walsh completed his urology residency and an endocrinology fellowship at the UCLA School of Medicine under Willard E. Goodwin (1967-1971). Goodwin was one of the pioneers of renal transplantation and reconstructive urology at UCLA. Walsh's training path: Case Western Reserve MD (1964), Peter Bent Brigham Hospital surgery residency (Boston), Children's Hospital Medical Center pediatric surgery (Boston), UCLA urology residency and endocrinology fellowship. He was recruited to Hopkins in 1974 at age 35 to direct the Brady Urological Institute after Ben Gittes (his San Diego mentor) declined the position. This edge uses the existing UCLA Department of Surgery institutional node, as urologic surgery at UCLA was administratively within the Department of Surgery. This is the first direct_training edge in the urology module, filling the most significant structural gap. Walsh was NOT Hopkins-trained — he represents an independent West Coast training lineage arriving at a Hopkins institution, paralleling Starzl's Hopkins-to-Pittsburgh path.

Walsh served as Chairman of the Brady Urological Institute from 1974 to 2004, transforming it from a declining program into the world's leading prostate cancer center. Case Western Reserve MD 1964, Peter Bent Brigham general surgery residency, UCLA urology residency — NOT Hopkins-trained; recruited externally. On April 26, 1982, he performed the first purposeful nerve-sparing radical prostatectomy, revolutionizing prostate cancer treatment. By 2002, prostate cancer deaths had declined 33%. He trained 62 residents who carried the technique worldwide and edited Campbell's Urology for 25 years (renamed Campbell-Walsh Urology in 2012). His directorship bridges the Hugh Hampton Young founding era (1915) to the modern era of the Brady.

evidence_locator: Johns Hopkins Brady Urological Institute History (hopkinsmedicine.org/brady-urology-institute/about-us/history)
notes: Walsh served as editor-in-chief of Campbell's Urology (the definitive urological textbook, now Campbell-Walsh-Wein Urology) for 25 years and was president of both the American Association of Genito-Urinary Surgeons and the Clinical Society of Genito-Urinary Surgeons. He was identified as the most cited urologist in the world in 2023. His nerve-sparing technique made radical prostatectomy the most common treatment for localized prostate cancer, fundamentally reshaping AUA practice guidelines.

target_node: Washington University Division of Urologic Surgery
notes: Clayman RV, Kavoussi LR, Soper NJ, et al. 'Laparoscopic nephrectomy: initial case report.' J Urol. 1991 Aug;146(2):278-82. Clayman joined Washington University in 1984 as faculty in the Division of Urologic Surgery, rising to Professor of Urology and Radiology and Director of the Midwest Stone Institute. On June 25, 1990, he and his team performed the world's first laparoscopic nephrectomy on an 85-year-old woman with a 3cm renal mass — the most transformative single procedure in urologic surgery since Walsh's nerve-sparing prostatectomy. He trained at UC San Diego (MD) and University of Minnesota (urology residency), where he was exposed to endourology by Arthur Smith. His WashU program became the global training epicenter for minimally invasive urologic surgery. This creates a completely independent endourology trunk parallel to the Brady/Hopkins lineage, based at WashU (which already has nodes for the Graham/cardiothoracic and Blair/plastic surgery lineages).

Tran MGB, Bhatt NR. 'Profile of Ralph Victor Clayman, MD: Professor and Chair, Department of Urology University of California Irvine.' J Endourol. 2010 Dec;24(12):1873-5. PMC3015495. Clayman co-founded the Endourological Society (exact founding year 1984) with Arthur D. Smith of Long Island Jewish Medical Center. The society publishes the Journal of Endourology (co-edited by Clayman and Smith) and is the global professional body for minimally invasive urologic surgery. The society now has chapters on every continent. This follows the established graph pattern of surgeon-innovators founding professional societies to disseminate their techniques (paralleling SAGES, SIS, EAST). The Endourological Society is the urologic equivalent of SAGES in general surgery.
